text: The surgeon applies a clamp to the renal artery, selectively occluding the vessel supplying the upper pole of the kidney, in preparation for removing a tumor located at the upper pole.

The surgeon identifies and occludes the feeding blood vessel to prevent bleeding during the procedure. The small tumor at the upper pole of the kidney is carefully peeled away from surrounding tissue, demonstrating a clean separation within the correct surgical plane. The excised tumor is temporarily placed aside for retrieval at the end of the surgery.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis by suturing the base of the kidney using a barbed V-lock suture with a large needle. The suture is snugged down without tying a knot, closing the defect efficiently. The clamp on the renal artery is released after two to three minutes to minimize kidney damage.

The surgeon restores blood flow to the kidney, with minimal bleeding observed due to the tumor's location at the edge of the kidney where blood vessels are sparse. A stitch is run up the length of the base to secure the area, ensuring the stitch holds firmly without slippage.

The surgeon uses a hemlock clip to snug the stitch down, then continues running the stitch to close the outer surface of the kidney. The barbs on the stitch help hold the kidney tissue together, and the hemlock clip allows for adjusting the tension on the stitch.
